title: Web APIs with FastAPI
subtitle: Spring Boot REST, with type hints doing the work

sections.content: FastAPI is the Python web framework a Spring Boot developer feels at home in: declarative routes, dependency injection, and validation — but powered by type hints and pydantic (Module 23). Install into your venv: pip install fastapi uvicorn, then run with uvicorn main:app --reload.

sections.java: // Spring Boot REST controller @RestController @RequestMapping("/items") public class ItemController { @GetMapping("/{id}") public Item get(@PathVariable Long id) { return service.find(id); } }
sections.python: # FastAPI app + path operation from fastapi import FastAPI app = FastAPI() @app.get("/items/{id}") def get_item(id: int) -> Item: return service.find(id) # id is parsed & validated as int from the path

sections.title: Type Hints Drive Routing, Validation, and Docs
sections.content: FastAPI reads your signature: id: int parses and validates the path param (a bad value returns a clean 422, like @Valid failing). A pydantic model parameter becomes the validated JSON body. Best of all, FastAPI auto-generates interactive OpenAPI/Swagger docs at /docs — the Springdoc experience, but free and always in sync, because the types are the schema.

sections.java: // Body binding + DI + query params @PostMapping public Item create( @Valid @RequestBody ItemIn body, @RequestParam(defaultValue = "false") boolean dry, Db db) { // injected bean return db.save(body); }
sections.python: # body = pydantic model, dep = Depends() from fastapi import Depends @app.post("/items") def create( body: ItemIn, # validated JSON body dry: bool = False, # query param ?dry= db: Db = Depends(get_db), # injected dependency ) -> Item: return db.save(body)

sections.title: Depends() Is FastAPI's @Autowired
sections.content: Depends(get_db) declares a dependency that FastAPI resolves and injects per request — your DB session, current user, settings, etc. Dependencies can themselves depend on others (a graph, like Spring beans), and a dependency that yields runs teardown after the response (Module 19's context-manager pattern). FastAPI is fully async: declare async def handlers to await DB/HTTP calls without blocking the event loop (Module 08).

sections.question: How does FastAPI know to validate an incoming JSON body and generate its schema?
options: You write a separate OpenAPI YAML file
From a pydantic-model-typed parameter on the handler — the type hints are the schema
It validates everything as strings by default
sections.explanation: Declaring a parameter typed as a pydantic BaseModel tells FastAPI to parse and validate the request body against it (returning 422 on bad data) and to publish that structure in the auto-generated OpenAPI docs at /docs. The type hints do the work — no separate schema file, no @RequestBody needed.
